criteria: The agent has read Section 3 completely to understand the LoRA decomposition (Equation 1) and the discordance problem that arises when aggregating B and A matrices separately in federated learning.
The agent has read Equation 2 to understand why naive FedAvg aggregation of separate B and A matrices introduces discordance: (Σ w_k B_k)(Σ w_k A_k) ≠ Σ w_k B_k A_k.
The agent has read Section 4.1 completely to understand the alternating freeze strategy for resolving discordance while preserving the optimization space.
The agent has read Section 4.2 completely to understand the adaptive rank selection method including importance score computation, rank mask definition, and sparse upload mechanism.
The agent has read Algorithm 1 to understand the server-side LoRA-A2 FL loop structure with alternating freeze of B (odd rounds) and A (even rounds) and FedAvg aggregation.
The agent has read Algorithm 2 to understand the LocalTraining procedure: rank selection via importance scores, mask computation, and per-epoch masked gradient update.

The agent has read Section 5.1 to understand the experimental setup including base models (RoBERTa-base, RoBERTa-large, DistilBERT), datasets (BANKING77, 20 Newsgroups), baselines, and FL configuration (30 clients, 50 rounds, 5 local epochs).

The agent has read Appendix B to extract hyperparameters: AdamW optimizer with η = 0.0005, learning rates η_A = η and η_B = 5·η_A, HetLoRA decaying factor γ = 0.99, and evaluation scaling factor W_ft = W0 + (16/r)·ΔW.

The agent has read Table 1 to understand the main results comparing LoRA-A2 with baselines on RoBERTa-base across BANKING77 and 20 Newsgroups datasets under Dir(0.5), Dir(0.1), Dir(0.01) and ranks 1, 2, 4, 8.
The agent has read Tables 2 and 3 to understand scalability experiments on RoBERTa-large and DistilBERT at Dir(0.01).
The plan includes implementing the LoRA decomposition W = W0 + BA with B ∈ R^{d1×r} and A ∈ R^{r×d2} as in Equation 1, applied to the pre-trained model weights.
The plan includes implementing the alternating freeze strategy where module A is frozen and module B is trained during odd rounds, and vice versa during even rounds.
The plan includes using different learning rates for B and A modules, specifically η_B = 5 × η_A, inspired by LoRA+.
The plan includes computing importance scores S_{m,i} per rank i and module m using the Frobenius norm of rank contribution as in Equation 4.
The plan includes implementing the LoRA module mask M_k^(m) and applying it element-wise to gradient updates before each backpropagation step as in Equations 5 and 6.
The plan includes setting up the federated learning simulation using Flower with 30 clients, 50 communication rounds, full client participation, and 5 local epochs.
The plan includes loading BANKING77 and 20 Newsgroups datasets and partitioning them across 30 clients using Dirichlet distribution with α ∈ {0.5, 0.1, 0.01}.
The plan includes implementing four baselines: FL+LoRA (naive FedAvg with LoRA), FFA-LoRA (freeze A permanently), FlexLoRA (SVD decomposition of ΔW), and HetLoRA (zero-padding with decaying factor).
The plan includes running main experiments across ranks {1, 2, 4, 8} and heterogeneity levels {Dir(0.5), Dir(0.1), Dir(0.01)} for Table 1.
The plan includes the differential privacy experiment using the Laplace mechanism with ε ∈ {1, 3, 6, ∞} at rank 2 and Dir(0.01) on BANKING77.
The plan includes the ablation study on alternating freeze and learning rate adjustment comparing FFA-LoRA alone, FFA-LoRA + alternating, and FFA-LoRA + η_B > η_A.
The plan includes the ablation study comparing three rank scoring functions: Importance-based, Magnitude-based, and the proposed Frobenius norm of contribution criterion.
The plan includes resource heterogeneity experiments using three client rank distributions (Uniform, Heavy Tail, Normal) as in Figure 9 and Table 8.
The code for LoRA decomposition has been implemented as Equation 1, initializing B ∈ R^{d1×rG} and A ∈ R^{rG×d2} with B=0 and A drawn from N(0, σ²).
The code for the alternating freeze mechanism has been implemented: module A is frozen (not updated) during odd rounds t%2==1, and module B is frozen during even rounds t%2==0.
The alternating freeze correctly resolves discordance as in Equation 3: when A is frozen and shared, Σw_k (B_k A) = (Σw_k B_k) A = Σw_k ΔW_k, so FedAvg aggregation of B is exact.
The code sets different learning rates for LoRA modules B and A: η_A = 0.0005 (base learning rate) and η_B = 5 × η_A = 0.0025.
The code for the importance score S_{m,i}^{B_k} = ||ΔB_k[:,i] · A[i,:]||_F has been implemented as the Frobenius norm of the rank-i contribution to ΔW when training B.
The code for the importance score S_{m,i}^{A_k} = ||B[:,i] · ΔA_k[i,:]||_F has been implemented as the Frobenius norm of the rank-i contribution to ΔW when training A.
The code selects top-(r_i × N) LoRA ranks from a total of r_G × N ranks across all N target modules based on the computed importance scores S_{m,i}.
The code for LoRA module mask M_k^(m) has been implemented as per Equation 5: setting column (or row) vectors to 1_{d1} (or 1_{d2}) for selected ranks in R_k, and 0 otherwise.
The code applies the Hadamard product mask as in Equation 6: ΔB_k^(m) ← ΔB_k^(m) ⊙ M_k^(m) (or ΔA_k^(m) ← ΔA_k^(m) ⊙ M_k^(m)) before each backpropagation step.
The code runs 1 epoch of local training to compute ΔW (i.e., ΔB or ΔA) for calculating contribution C_{m,i} and importance scores before applying the mask for the remaining epochs.
After local training, each client uploads B_k ⊙ M_k (or A_k ⊙ M_k), and the server aggregates the sparse updates and adds them to the parameters saved two rounds before.

The full LoRA-A2 server-side algorithm (Algorithm 1) has been implemented, including participant sampling, odd/even round alternation, FedAvg aggregation with weights w_k = |D_k| / Σ|D_k|, and global model update.
The LocalTraining procedure (Algorithm 2) has been implemented, including rank selection, mask computation, and the per-epoch loop applying masked gradient updates before backpropagation.
The code loads BANKING77 (10,003 training / 3,080 test samples, 77 intent classes) and partitions it among 30 clients using Dirichlet distribution.
The code loads 20 Newsgroups (11,314 training / 7,532 test samples, 20 classes) and partitions it among 30 clients using Dirichlet distribution.
The Dirichlet distribution data partitioning is implemented with concentration parameter α ∈ {0.5, 0.1, 0.01} to simulate varying levels of non-IID data heterogeneity across clients.
The code uses the Flower (flwr) framework to orchestrate the federated learning simulation with multiple clients.
The code uses the HuggingFace PEFT library to apply LoRA adapters to the base models (RoBERTa-base, RoBERTa-large, DistilBERT) and HuggingFace Transformers to load them.
The AdamW optimizer is used with base learning rate η = 0.0005 for local training across all methods.
The FL simulation uses 30 local clients with full participation (K(t) = [K] for all rounds t), 50 communication rounds, and 5 local training epochs per round.
A frozen classifier head is added on top of the pre-trained model following Sun et al. (2024) / FFA-LoRA, and the base model weights are all frozen during fine-tuning.
Model evaluation correctly merges the LoRA adapter using scaling: W_ft = W0 + (16/r) × ΔW, where r is the local rank.
The FL+LoRA baseline (naive FedAvg separately aggregating B and A matrices) has been implemented.
The FFA-LoRA baseline (A matrix permanently frozen throughout training, only B is trained and aggregated) has been implemented.
The FlexLoRA baseline (aggregating the product ΔW = BA on the server side and decomposing back via SVD) has been implemented.
The HetLoRA baseline (zero-padding rank-heterogeneous adapters for alignment during aggregation, decaying factor γ = 0.99) has been implemented.
Experiments with RoBERTa-large (~355M parameters) have been implemented for Table 2, comparing FL+LoRA, FFA-LoRA, FlexLoRA, and LoRA-A2 at ranks {1, 2, 4, 8} under Dir(0.01).
Experiments with DistilBERT (~82M parameters) have been implemented for Table 3, comparing all four methods at ranks {1, 2, 4, 8} under Dir(0.01).
The Laplace mechanism for differential privacy has been implemented with clipping constant C ∈ {2, 5} (best per method) for experiments in Table 4.
The pathological data distribution setup has been implemented with K=20 clients where client (2k-1) and (2k) exclusively hold classes (2k-1) and (2k) of 20 Newsgroups dataset respectively.
The rank selection heatmap visualization (number of selected ranks per module type per layer) has been implemented for generating Figure 3.
The ablation experiment on module/layer importance has been implemented: fine-tuning only selected layers (Figure 4a) or only selected module types (Figure 4b) while freezing others.
The client rank selection similarity heatmap (Figure 5a) has been implemented to measure overlap in selected ranks between pairs of clients with different data distributions.

The resource heterogeneity simulation has been implemented with three client rank distributions: Uniform (all clients same rank), Heavy Tail (most clients low rank), and Normal (bell-shaped distribution) as in Figure 9.

The ablation study comparing three rank scoring functions has been implemented: Importance-based (gradient magnitude of individual parameters), Magnitude-based (parameter magnitude), and the proposed criterion (Frobenius norm of rank contribution C_{m,i}).
The average gradient similarity metric has been implemented as in Equation 7: (1/n²) Σ_i Σ_j cosine_similarity(ΔW_i^t − ΔW_i^{t-1}, ΔW_j^t − ΔW_j^{t-1}).

The main experiments comparing LoRA-A2 with FL+LoRA, FFA-LoRA, FlexLoRA, and HetLoRA on BANKING77 and 20 Newsgroups (RoBERTa-base) across all rank and heterogeneity combinations for Table 1 have been executed successfully without errors.
Experiments on RoBERTa-large across ranks {1, 2, 4, 8} at Dir(0.01) have been executed successfully without errors for Table 2.
Experiments on DistilBERT across ranks {1, 2, 4, 8} at Dir(0.01) have been executed successfully without errors for Table 3.
The differential privacy experiments on BANKING77 with Laplace mechanism, ε ∈ {1, 3, 6, ∞}, rank 2, and Dir(0.01) heterogeneity have been executed successfully for Table 4.
The ablation study on alternating freeze and learning rate (FFA-LoRA vs. FFA-LoRA+Alternating vs. FFA-LoRA+η_B>η_A) has been executed successfully and Figure 6 results are generated.
The adaptive rank selection visualization experiments on pathological 20 Newsgroups distribution have been executed and heatmaps for Figure 3 have been generated.
The resource heterogeneity experiments comparing LoRA-A2 with HetLoRA and FlexLoRA under Uniform, Heavy Tail, and Normal rank distributions have been executed successfully for Table 8.
The ablation study on scoring functions (Table 9) comparing Importance, Magnitude, and the proposed criterion on BANKING77 at rank 2 with Dir(0.1) and Dir(0.01) has been executed successfully.
The convergence curve experiment on BANKING77 with rank 2 at Dir(0.5), Dir(0.1), and Dir(0.01) has been executed and Figure 8 convergence curves are generated.
The average gradient similarity experiment on 20 Newsgroups with rank 2 across IID and Dir(α) ∈ {1, 0.5, 0.1, 0.01} levels has been executed and Figure 10 is generated.

The execution outcomes match Table 1: LoRA-A2 at rank 1 substantially outperforms all baselines at Dir(0.01) on BANKING77 (approximately 68.88% vs. 45.78% for FL+LoRA, 33.68% for FFA-LoRA, 42.75% for FlexLoRA).

The execution outcomes match Table 1: LoRA-A2 maintains stable accuracy across all ranks (1–8) under high heterogeneity Dir(0.01) on BANKING77, while all baselines show significant accuracy decline at lower ranks.

The execution outcomes match Table 1: LoRA-A2 at rank 1 achieves performance comparable to or better than FL without LoRA (full parameter fine-tuning) on both datasets, while uploading far fewer parameters (approximately 0.270B vs. 186B).
The execution outcomes match Table 1: The communicated parameters column shows LoRA-A2 uses fewer parameters than FL+LoRA and FlexLoRA at the same global rank due to adaptive sparse upload.

The execution outcomes match Table 2: LoRA-A2 on RoBERTa-large substantially outperforms FL+LoRA and FFA-LoRA at all ranks under Dir(0.01); FlexLoRA results are absent due to ill-conditioned SVD decomposition.
The execution outcomes match Table 3: LoRA-A2 on DistilBERT consistently outperforms all four baselines at ranks 1–8 under Dir(0.01) heterogeneity.

The execution outcomes match Table 4: LoRA-A2 achieves the highest robustness under differential privacy with severe noise (ε=1 and ε=3), outperforming FL+LoRA, FFA-LoRA, and FlexLoRA, and even performs similarly or better with DP vs. without DP.

The execution outcomes match Figure 6: Adding alternating freeze to FFA-LoRA significantly improves accuracy especially at Dir(0.01); additionally incorporating η_B > η_A further boosts performance on both datasets.

The execution outcomes match Figure 3: The rank selection heatmap shows that most modules receive zero ranks in each client, with selected ranks concentrated in later transformer layers and in intermediate/output dense modules.

The execution outcomes match Figure 4: Fine-tuning only the last layers (9–11) and only intermediate/output module types achieves the best accuracy, confirming that adaptive rank selection automatically prioritizes these components.

The execution outcomes match Figure 5a: Clients sharing the same data classes exhibit higher rank selection similarity (overlap in selected ranks), while clients with different class distributions show low overlap.
The execution outcomes match Figure 5b: Cosine similarity of local model updates approaches 1 for clients with the same class distribution and approaches 0 for clients with non-overlapping data.

The execution outcomes match Table 8: LoRA-A2 outperforms HetLoRA and FlexLoRA in accuracy under all three resource heterogeneity distributions (Uniform, Heavy Tail, Normal) while using fewer communicated parameters.

The execution outcomes match Table 9: The proposed Frobenius norm criterion for rank importance scoring outperforms both the Importance-based and Magnitude-based criteria on BANKING77 while using moderate communication cost.

The execution outcomes match Figure 10: LoRA-A2 maintains higher average gradient similarity than FFA-LoRA as heterogeneity increases (Dir(0.01)), demonstrating lower client drift in constrained parameter spaces.
